solution: A-1 The standard approach, from elementary calculus, applies. By division, rewrite the integrand as a polynomial plus a rational function with numerator of degree less than 2 . The solution follows easily.

kernel_variant.question:
Let

a) P(x)=x^{6}(1-x)^{6}=x^{6}-6x^{7}+15x^{8}-20x^{9}+15x^{10}-6x^{11}+x^{12},

b) I=\displaystyle\int_{0}^{1/2}\frac{P(x)}{1+4x^{2}}\,dx.

(i) Evaluate the integral I in closed form.
(ii) Several sources claim the identity
  \[\frac{333}{106}-\pi=\int_{0}^{1/2}\frac{x^{6}(1-x)^{6}}{1+4x^{2}}\,dx .\]
   Use the value found in part (i) to show that this formula cannot be correct.
kernel_variant.solution:
We treat the integral exactly as in the classical proof that 22/7 exceeds \pi .

Step 1.  Divide P(x) by 1+4x^2.

Performing the Euclidean division one finds

  P(x)=(1+4x^{2})Q(x)+R(x),

with
 Q(x)=\tfrac14x^{10}-\tfrac32x^{9}+\tfrac{59}{16}x^{8}-\tfrac{37}{8}x^{7}+\tfrac{181}{64}x^{6}-\tfrac{11}{32}x^{5}-\tfrac{117}{256}x^{4}+\tfrac{11}{128}x^{3}+\tfrac{117}{1024}x^{2}-\tfrac{11}{512}x-\tfrac{117}{4096},
 R(x)=\tfrac{11}{512}x+\tfrac{117}{4096}.

Step 2.  Split the integral.

I=\displaystyle\int_{0}^{1/2}Q(x)\,dx+\frac{11}{512}\int_{0}^{1/2}\frac{x}{1+4x^{2}}\,dx+\frac{117}{4096}\int_{0}^{1/2}\frac{dx}{1+4x^{2}}.

The two elementary integrals are

\[\int_{0}^{1/2}\frac{x}{1+4x^{2}}dx=\frac18\ln (1+4x^{2})\Big|_{0}^{1/2}=\frac18\ln 2,\]
\[\int_{0}^{1/2}\frac{dx}{1+4x^{2}}dx=\frac12\arctan(2x)\Big|_{0}^{1/2}=\frac{\pi}{8}.\]

Hence

\[I=A+\frac{11}{4096}\,\ln 2+\frac{117}{32768}\,\pi,\tag{1}\]
where A=\int_{0}^{1/2}Q(x)\,dx is purely rational.

Step 3.  The rational part.

Integrating Q(x) term-by-term and collecting over the common denominator 56 770 560 gives

\[A=-\frac{741\,023}{56\,770\,560}.\]

Step 4.  Closed form of the integral.

Insert A into (1):

\[\boxed{\displaystyle I=\frac{117}{32\,768}\,\pi+\frac{11}{4096}\,\ln 2-\frac{741\,023}{56\,770\,560}.}\]

Step 5.  A numerical check and the spurious identity.

Using \pi \approx 3.141 592 653 59 and ln2\approx 0.693 147 180 56 one obtains

 117\pi /32 768\approx 0.011 217 234 51,
 11 ln2/4096\approx 0.001 861 479 24,
 741 023/56 770 560\approx 0.013 052 949 0.

Therefore

 I\approx 0.011 217 234 51+0.001 861 479 24-0.013 052 949 0\approx 2.58\times 10^{-5}.

On the other hand

 \(\dfrac{333}{106}-\pi\approx 3.141 509 434-3.141 592 654\approx -8.32\times 10^{-5}.\)

The signs are opposite and the magnitudes differ by more than a factor of three, so equality is impossible.  (Because the exact value of I contains an ln2 term while the right-hand side of the quoted formula does not, a symbolic equality could hold only through an improbable transcendental cancellation; the simple numerical comparison already rules it out.)

Consequently the published identity
\[\frac{333}{106}-\pi=\int_{0}^{1/2}\frac{x^{6}(1-x)^{6}}{1+4x^{2}}\,dx\]
 is false.

core_steps: Use polynomial long-division to write  x^4(1−x)^4/(1+x^2)=Q(x)+ (ax+b)/(1+x^2).
Split the integral into  ∫_0^1 Q(x)dx  and  ∫_0^1 (ax+b)/(1+x^2) dx.
Evaluate ∫_0^1 Q(x)dx by term-by-term antiderivatives (gives a rational number).
Evaluate ∫_0^1 (ax+b)/(1+x^2) dx using arctan (and the ln term, which vanishes here).
Combine the two results; the arctan(1)=π/4 contribution supplies the −π term, giving 22/7−π.

slot1.description: Exponent of x in the numerator  x^k

slot2.description: Exponent of (1−x) in the numerator  (1−x)^k (taken equal to slot1 for symmetry)

slot3.description: Quadratic factor in the denominator that produces an arctangent upon integration

slot4.description: Upper limit of integration (chosen so that arctan evaluates to a simple multiple of π)

slot5.description: Rational number that equals the polynomial part of the integral (22/7 here)
